common_name: Southern Wood Fern (River Fern, Kunth Maiden Fern)

planting.note: Plant container stock or rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; southern wood fern is native to moist, wooded stream banks and shaded bottomlands from South Carolina to Texas and is exceptionally well-adapted to Gulf Coast shade gardens — thriving in the heat and humidity of zone 9.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in part to full shade; keep consistently moist. Thelypteris kunthii (southern wood fern or river fern) is the most commonly encountered native fern of the Gulf Coastal Plain understory — a vigorous, deciduous-to-semi-evergreen, colony-forming fern found naturally along shaded stream banks, bottomland forests, and moist woodland margins from the Carolinas through Florida and west to Texas. The fronds are once-pinnate (single-divided) with narrow, soft, light-to-medium-green leaflets and a somewhat delicate texture, produced in graceful, arching, spreading colonies that can cover large areas of shaded ground. Unlike many ferns that struggle in the heat and humidity of southern summers, T. kunthii actively thrives in Gulf Coast conditions, making it an outstanding — and underused — native groundcover for shaded areas. It spreads moderately by underground rhizomes to form weed-suppressing colonies. In zone 9, fronds may go partially or fully dormant in a hard freeze but the plant resproutes vigorously in spring. As a low-maintenance native shade groundcover, southern wood fern has few equals for moist, shaded sites in the Gulf South.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Ferns
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Shade Ferns

common_name: Downy Wood Fern (Tropical Woodfern, Shield Fern)

planting.note: Plant container stock or divisions in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in full shade to part shade; downy wood fern is a naturalized tropical fern common across south Florida, the Gulf Coast, and tropical regions — it spreads rapidly and can form dense colonies in moist, shaded conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in full shade to part shade; downy wood fern spreads readily from spores and can naturalize in suitable conditions. Thelypteris dentata (downy wood fern or tropical woodfern) is an Old World tropical fern now naturalized across the Gulf Coastal Plain, Florida, Louisiana, and humid tropical regions of North America. A large, coarse-textured, rapidly spreading fern, it produces lush colonies of once-pinnate fronds 18–30 inches long with broadly lobed, soft-hairy leaflets giving the plant its 'downy' descriptor — the undersurface of the fronds has a distinctly soft, hairy texture when touched. In zone 8–12 shaded gardens, downy wood fern is one of the easiest and most heat-tolerant ferns to grow — it thrives where other ferns fail and spreads vigorously enough to function as a low-maintenance shade groundcover. It tolerates dry shade better than most ferns once established. While sometimes considered a weedy volunteer in tropical gardens (it self-propagates readily from windblown spores), it is genuinely useful for covering large, difficult shaded areas with minimal care. Some authorities include it within the broader Thelypteris palustris complex.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Shade Ferns

planting.note: Plant in moist, coarse, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, airy substrate in bright indirect light to deep shade; crocodile fern is an epiphytic Southeast Asian fern best grown in loose, fast-draining, bark-based substrate — treat similarly to an orchid in terms of substrate aeration and avoid dense, waterlogged potting media.

directions: Grow in coarse, airy, humus-rich, well-drained substrate in bright indirect light to shade; allow the top inch to dry slightly between waterings. Microsorum musifolium (crocodile fern or alligator fern) is a distinctive Malaysian and Southeast Asian epiphytic fern producing broad, undivided (simple), banana-leaf-like fronds 12–24 inches long with an extraordinary textural pattern: the entire surface of each pale-to-medium green frond is marked with an intricate network of darker-green veins in a hexagonal, honeycomb-like pattern that uncannily resembles the skin pattern of a crocodile — giving the plant its striking common name and making it one of the most visually interesting tropical foliage plants. Like bird nest ferns, it grows naturally as an epiphyte in the forks of trees in humid tropical forests; in cultivation it prefers a coarse, open, well-aerated substrate rather than dense potting soil. As a houseplant or shade garden plant (in frost-free zones), it is a conversation piece with its uniquely patterned fronds. It requires high humidity and protection from temperatures below 55°F and is best suited to zone 10–12 outdoor gardens or indoor/greenhouse growing.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Tropical Ferns

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in spring after soil warms in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moist soil in full sun to part shade; waterleaf is a fast-growing tropical leafy vegetable that thrives in the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers and can be harvested as a cut-and-come-again crop from 30 days.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in warm soil in full sun to part shade in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; harvest outer leaves regularly to encourage continued production. Talinum fruticosum (waterleaf, Philippine spinach, or Lagos spinach) is a widespread tropical leafy vegetable native to South America but naturalized and cultivated throughout the tropics of Asia, Africa, and the Caribbean — it is one of the most important leafy green vegetables in West African cuisines (especially Nigerian cooking, where it is called 'waterleaf' or 'gbure') and is increasingly grown in Southeast Asian communities across the United States. The thick, succulent, slightly mucilaginous leaves are highly nutritious (rich in protein, iron, calcium, and vitamins) and are cooked in soups, stews, and stir-fries much like spinach — they have a mild, pleasant flavor that intensifies slightly when cooked. The plant produces cheerful, tiny, bright-pink, five-petaled flowers continuously through the growing season. It is extremely heat-tolerant and productive during Gulf Coast summers when cool-season greens have long expired, filling the important summer greens gap in zone 9 gardens. Self-seeds readily in warm climates.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Leafy Vegetables
Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Talinum

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–October in zone 9) in well-drained to dry, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; okahijiki is a cool-season Japanese saltwort vegetable grown for its tender, bright-green, spiny young shoots — it is an unusual cool-weather specialty vegetable that tolerates salty, lean soils and grows in conditions where most vegetables fail.

directions: Direct sow seeds shallowly in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun in fall or early spring; thin to 3–4 inches. Salsola komarovii (okahijiki or land seaweed) is a Japanese saltwort vegetable — an annual succulent herb related to the Russian thistle (tumbleweed), grown in Japan for its tender, crisp, spiny young shoots that are bright green, cylindrical, and highly reminiscent in texture and mild, slightly salty flavor of sea vegetables (seaweed). The 'okahijiki' name literally means 'land hijiki,' comparing the plant to hijiki (a Japanese seaweed); the 'land seaweed' comparison reflects both the plant's mild marine-like flavor and its coastal saltmarsh habitat. Young shoots are harvested at 3–6 inches when most tender and used in salads, blanched as a side vegetable, or incorporated into Japanese cuisine as a seasonal vegetable; the texture is pleasantly crisp and slightly mucilaginous. The plant is extremely drought-tolerant and grows in salty, lean, sandy soils where most vegetables fail. As it matures, it becomes increasingly spiny and fibrous; harvest young for best eating quality. An unusual and underutilized cool-season specialty vegetable for adventurous gardeners.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Asian Vegetables
